critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression 30-day readmission design contains several issues that undermine the comparison.

1. INTENTIONAL FEATURE ASYMMETRY INVALIDATES ARCHITECTURE COMPARISON: The RNN receives full visit sequences with temporal ordering; the logistic regression receives manually engineered aggregate statistics (counts, binary indicators). The design explicitly states this difference is expected and part of the hypothesis. But this means the experiment cannot attribute a performance difference to architecture — it attributes it jointly to feature richness and architecture. The RNN has access to visit-level details (individual diagnoses, visit type) that the logistic regression never sees, regardless of architectural choice. A proper test of sequential modeling would require giving both models access to the same information and varying only the architecture.

2. LEFT-PADDING OF VARIABLE-LENGTH SEQUENCES: The design pads short sequences with zeros at the left (beginning of sequence). For an LSTM processing left-to-right, this means the model processes many zero-vector timesteps before reaching real visits. Zeros will drive the LSTM hidden state toward its zero-input fixed point before any informative signals are seen. The alternative — right-padding and masking — is more appropriate. The design does not specify whether masking is applied to padded positions.

3. 10,000 ADMISSIONS IN 24 MONTHS — PATIENT REPEAT ADMISSION OVERLAP: The design assigns patients to splits by index admission date. However, a patient with an index admission in month 11 (training) and a subsequent admission in month 14 (validation) will have their training-window visit history included when constructing the validation sequence feature, because the RNN uses the 12-month prior visit history for any admission. If a patient's index admission is in the validation set but their prior visits are in training, those prior visits are fine. But if a patient has multiple index admissions (one in training and one in validation), the design does not specify how this is handled.

4. PREVALENCE DRIFT HANDLING IS PASSIVE: The design checks for readmission prevalence drift and reports it as a confound. But if prevalence changes significantly between the 12-month training window and the 6-month test window, the class imbalance weighting (weight_1 = n_neg / n_pos from training) will be miscalibrated for the test period, systematically biasing the predicted probabilities. Reporting drift as a confound rather than adjusting for it means the AUROC and calibration metrics in the test period are evaluated under a different prior than the model was trained to expect.
all_issues_raised: Intentional feature asymmetry between RNN (sequences) and logistic regression (aggregates) makes architecture the confounded variable
Left-padding of variable-length sequences without masking drives LSTM hidden state through uninformative zero-vector timesteps
Multiple admissions per patient across split boundaries is not addressed, risking implicit feature leakage
Readmission prevalence drift in test period miscalibrates class weights fitted on training, biasing probability estimates
One-hot encoding of top-50 ICD codes with 'other' pooling removes signal from rare high-risk conditions; comparison partly measures handling of 'other' category
MLP baseline receives flattened unordered features, not order-preserving summaries; cannot distinguish capacity from sequencing effects
12-month training window may be insufficient for learning multi-year clinical deterioration patterns; LSTM parameter count is high relative to training examples
Label auditing correction mechanism is not described; high-confidence sensitivity analysis may reflect non-representative subset of easily verifiable cases
RNN vs. logistic regression comparison conflates sequential modeling capacity with feature granularity; logistic regression receives count aggregates while RNN receives visit-level codes
Class-weighted LSTM training uncalibrates probability outputs; reported calibration metrics will be inflated without post-hoc recalibration
Asymmetric hyperparameter tuning: logistic regression uses nested CV on training data while LSTM hyperparameters are tuned on the held-out validation set
Sequence truncation at 95th percentile systematically removes early visit history for the highest-risk (most frequent-visiting) patients
